cypher:
MATCH
    p1 = (m:Movie)-[r1:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l1:Language),
    p2 = (m)-[r2:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l2:Language),
    p3 = (m)-[r3:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c1:Country),
    p4 = (m)-[r4:IN_GENRE]->(g1:Genre),
    p5 = (m)-[r5:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc1:ProductionCompany),
    p6 = (m)-[r6:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k1:Keyword),
    p7 = (m)-[r7:IN_COLLECTION]->(col1:Collection),
    p8 = (p)-[r8:CAST_FOR]->(p2:Person),
    p9 = (p)-[r9:CREW_FOR]->(p3:Movie),
    p10 = (p)-[r10:CREW_FOR]->(p4:Video),
    p11 = (u)-[r11:RATED]->(m),
    p12 = (u)-[r12:RATED]->(v:Video),
    p13 = (v)-[r13:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l3:Language),
    p14 = (v)-[r14:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l4:Language),
    p15 = (v)-[r15:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c2:Country),
    p16 = (v)-[r16:IN_GENRE]->(g2:Genre),
    p17 = (v)-[r17:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc2:ProductionCompany),
    p18 = (v)-[r18:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k2:Keyword),
    p19 = (v)-[r19:IN_COLLECTION]->(col2:Collection),
    p20 = (a)-[r20:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l5:Language),
    p21 = (a)-[r21:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l6:Language),
    p22 = (a)-[r22:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c3:Country),
    p23 = (a)-[r23:IN_GENRE]->(g3:Genre),
    p24 = (a)-[r24:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc3:ProductionCompany),
    p25 = (a)-[r25:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k3:Keyword),
    p26 = (a)-[r26:IN_COLLECTION]->(col3:Collection),
    p27 = (pk)-[r27:PROVIDES_ACCESS_TO]->(g4:Genre),
    p28 = (sk)-[r28:FOR_PACKAGE]->(pk:Package)
WHERE
    // Match the same Movie node for all paths
    p1.m == p2.m == p3.m == p4.m == p5.m == p6.m == p7.m ==
    p8.p == p9.p == p10.p == p11.p == p12.p == p13.p == p14.p ==
    p15.p == p16.p == p17.p == p18.p == p19.p == p20.p ==
    p21.p == p22.p == p23.p == p24.p == p25.p == p26.p == p27.p == p28.p;

RETURN
    DISTINCT
    m,
    l1.name AS original_language,
    l2.name AS spoken_language,
    c1.name AS production_country,
    g1.name AS genre,
    pc1.name AS producer,
    k1.name AS keyword,
    col1.name AS collection,
    // Cast and Crew details
    [node in nodes(p8) | node.name] AS cast,
    [node in nodes(p9) | node.name] AS crew_movie,
    [node in nodes(p10) | node.name] AS crew_video,
    // Ratings
    [r11.rating] AS movie_rating,
    [r12.rating] AS video_rating,
    // Video details
    l3.name AS video_original_language,
    l4.name AS video_spoken_language,
    c2.name AS video_production_country,
    g2.name AS video_genre,
    pc2.name AS video_producer,
    k2.name AS video_keyword,
    col2.name AS video_collection,
    // Adult details
    l5.name AS adult_original_language,
    l6.name AS adult_spoken_language,
    c3.name AS adult_production_country,
    g3.name AS adult_genre,
    pc3.name AS adult_producer,
    k3.name AS adult_keyword,
    col3.name AS adult_collection,
    // Package and Genre details
    g4.name AS package_accessible_genre,
    pk.name AS package_name,
    sk.name AS subscription_name,
    sk.expiresAt AS subscription_expires_at;
question:
Here’s the refined Cypher query based on your requirements, ensuring clarity and correctness for extracting structured data from movies, videos, and adult content with their associated metadata, cast, crew, ratings, and package/subscription details:

---
### **Optimized Query for Structured Metadata Extraction**
```cypher
MATCH
    // Core Movie Data
    (m:Movie)-[r1:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l1:Language),
    (m)-[r2:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l2:Language),
    (m)-[r3:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c1:Country),
    (m)-[r4:IN_GENRE]->(g1:Genre),
    (m)-[r5:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc1:ProductionCompany),
    (m)-[r6:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k1:Keyword),
    (m)-[r7:IN_COLLECTION]->(col1:Collection),

    // Cast and Crew
    (p)-[r8:CAST_FOR]->(m) AS cast_nodes,
    (p)-[r9:CREW_FOR]->(m) AS crew_nodes,

    // Ratings
    (u)-[r10:RATED]->(m) AS rated_movies,

    // Video Data
    (v:Video)-[r11:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l3:Language),
    (v)-[r12:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l4:Language),
    (v)-[r13:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c2:Country),
    (v)-[r14:IN_GENRE]->(g2:Genre),
    (v)-[r15:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc2:ProductionCompany),
    (v)-[r16:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k2:Keyword),
    (v)-[r17:IN_COLLECTION]->(col2:Collection),

    // Adult Data
    (a:Adult)-[r18:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l5:Language),
    (a)-[r19:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l6:Language),
    (a)-[r20:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c3:Country),
    (a)-[r21:IN_GENRE]->(g3:Genre),
    (a)-[r22:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc3:ProductionCompany),
    (a)-[r23:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k3:Keyword),
    (a)-[r24:IN_COLLECTION]->(col3:Collection),

    // Package/Subscription Details
    (pk)-[r25:PROVIDES_ACCESS_TO]->(g4:Genre),
    (sk)-[r26:FOR_PACKAGE]->(pk)
WHERE
    // Match the same Movie/Videos/Adult nodes
    m == v == a &&
    // Ensure cast and crew are distinct from the movie node
    p != m &&
    // Exclude self-references in cast/crew
    NOT EXISTS { (p)-[:CAST_FOR]->(m) | p = m } &&
    NOT EXISTS { (p)-[:CREW_FOR]->(m) | p = m }

RETURN
    DISTINCT
    m.title AS movie_title,
    m.year AS movie_year,

    // Language
    l1.name AS original_language,
    l2.name AS spoken_language,

    // Production
    c1.name AS production_country,
    pc1.name AS producer,

    // Genre
    g1.name AS genre,

    // Keywords/Collections
    collect(DISTINCT k1.name) AS keywords,
    collect(DISTINCT col1.name) AS collections,

    // Cast
    collect(DISTINCT node.name FROM nodes(cast_nodes)) AS cast_members,

    // Crew
    collect(DISTINCT node.name FROM nodes(crew_nodes)) AS crew_members,

    // Ratings (if any)
    collect(DISTINCT r10.rating) AS movie_ratings,

    // Video Data
    v.title AS video_title,
    v.year AS video_year,
    l3.name AS video_original_language,
    l4.name AS video_spoken_language,
    c2.name AS video_production_country,
    g2.name AS video_genre,
    collect(DISTINCT k2.name) AS video_keywords,
    collect(DISTINCT col2.name) AS video_collections,

    // Adult Data
    a.title AS adult_title,
    a.year AS adult_year,
    l5.name AS adult_original_language,
    l6.name AS adult_spoken_language,
    c3.name AS adult_production_country,
    g3.name AS adult_genre,
    collect(DISTINCT k3.name) AS adult_keywords,
    collect(DISTINCT col3.name) AS adult_collections,

    // Package/Subscription
    g4.name AS package_accessible_genre,
    pk.name AS package_name,
    sk.name AS subscription_name,
    sk.expiresAt AS subscription_expires_at;
```

---

### **Key Improvements:**
1. **Distinct Nodes**:
   - Ensures `m`, `v`, and `a` are unique (movies, videos, adults).
   - Excludes self-references in cast/crew relationships.

2. **Structured Aggregations**:
   - Uses `collect(DISTINCT ...)` for keywords/collections (handles duplicates).
   - Returns flat lists for cast/crew members.

3. **Explicit Filters**:
   - Excludes redundant paths (e.g., `p != m` to avoid self-references).

4. **Readability**:
   - Descriptive aliases (`l1`, `l2`, etc.) for clarity.
   - Grouped by **Movie**, **Video**, and **Adult** sections.

---

### **Example Output:**
For a query like:
```cypher
MATCH (m:Movie) WHERE m.title = 'Inception'
RETURN ...;
```
The result might look like:
```json
{
  "movie_title": "Inception",
  "original_language": "English",
  "production_country": "USA",
  "cast_members": ["Leonardo DiCaprio", "Joseph Gordon-Levitt"],
  "keywords": ["Sci-Fi", "Thriller"],
  "package_accessible_genre": "Action-Adventure"
}
```

question:
Here’s a refined and well-structured Cypher query that addresses your intent (finding connections related to movies/actors/genres) while optimizing for clarity and efficiency:

---
### **Query: Find Connections in Movie/Video Ecosystem**
```cypher
MATCH
  // Core: Find movies/ads by title
  (m:Movie {title: $title}) <-
    // Explore all possible relationships (cast, crew, keywords, etc.)
    [r:CAST_FOR | CREW_FOR | IN_GENRE | HAS_KEYWORD | IN_COLLECTION | PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY | ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE] *0..10 - (any:Movie|Person|Genre|Country|Language|Collection)
  RETURN
    m,
    [n IN nodes(r) | n] AS related_nodes,
    [e IN edges(r) | e] AS relationships;
```

---
### **Key Features:**
1. **Flexible Paths**: Matches up to 10 steps (`*0..10`) for deep exploration.
2. **All Relationship Types**: Covers `CAST_FOR`, `CREW_FOR`, `IN_GENRE`, etc.
3. **Returns Nodes/Edges**: Lists connected entities (actors, genres, collections) and their relationships.

---

### **Example Usage:**
```cypher
MATCH
  (m:Movie {title: "Inception"}) <-
    [r:CAST_FOR | CREW_FOR | IN_GENRE | HAS_KEYWORD] *0..3 - (any)
RETURN m, [n IN nodes(r) | n] AS related_entities, [e IN edges(r) | e] AS connections;
```
**Output:**
- `Inception` + actors (Leonardo DiCaprio, Joseph Gordon-Levitt) + genres (Sci-Fi/Action) + keywords (dreaming).

---

### **Optimized for Specific Use Cases:**
1. **Find Cast of a Movie**:
   ```cypher
   MATCH (m:Movie {title: "Inception"}) <-[:CAST_FOR] - (p:Person)
   RETURN p.name AS actor;
   ```
2. **Find Genres of a Movie**:
   ```cypher
   MATCH (m:Movie {title: "Inception"}) <-[:IN_GENRE] - (g:Genre)
   RETURN g.name AS genre;
   ```

---
### **Why This Works:**
- **Efficiency**: Limits depth (`*0..10`) to avoid infinite loops.
- **Clarity**: Separates nodes (`nodes(r)`) and edges (`edges(r)`) for readability.
- **Extensibility**: Add filters (e.g., `WHERE any.end.name = "Sci-Fi"`) to refine results.

MATCH

(:Movie)-[:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(:Language)

(:Movie)-[:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(:Country)

(:User)-[:RATED]->(:Video)

(:Video)-[:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(:Language)
(:Video)-[:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(:Country)
(:Video)-[:IN_GENRE]->(:Genre)
(:Video)-[:PRODUCED_BY]->(:ProductionCompany)
(:Video)-[:HAS_KEYWORD]->(:Keyword)
(:Video)-[:IN_COLLECTION]->(:Collection)
(:Adult)-[:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(:Language)
(:Adult)-[:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(:Language)
(:Adult)-[:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(:Country)
(:Adult)-[:IN_GENRE]->(:Genre)
(:Adult)-[:PRODUCED_BY]->(:ProductionCompany)
(:Adult)-[:HAS_KEYWORD]->(:Keyword)

cypher:
MATCH p = (m:Movie)-[r1:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE|PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY|IN_GENRE|PRODUCED_BY|HAS_KEYWORD|IN_COLLECTION]-(n:Language|Country|Genre|ProductionCompany|Keyword|Collection)
OPTIONAL MATCH p2 = (m)-[r2:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]-(p:Person)
OPTIONAL MATCH p3 = (m)-[r3:RATED]->(u:User)
OPTIONAL MATCH p4 = (m)-[r4:IN_COLLECTION]->(c:Collection) WHERE NOT EXISTS(p4)
WITH p, p2, p3, p4
MATCH (m)-[r5:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]->(p2:Person)
WHERE NOT EXISTS((m)-[r6:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]->(p2)) AND NOT EXISTS((m)-[r7:CREW_FOR]->(p2))
WITH p, p2, p3, p4, r5
RETURN m, collect(DISTINCT n) AS creators, collect(DISTINCT u) AS ratings, collect(DISTINCT c) AS collections, p2, r5;

MATCH
    p1 = (a:Character {name: 'Hamlet'}),

    p5 = (d)-[r4:INTERACTS2]->(e:Character {name: 'Goneril'}),
    p6 = (a)-[r5:INTERACTS1]->(f:Character {name: 'Fortinbras'}) WITH
    p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6 AS paths
WHERE
    size([x IN paths WHERE x[0] = a]) > 1
RETURN
    p1, p2, p4, p6;
question: How does Hamlet’s centrality in multiple literary interactions compare with his direct influence on Fortinbras and Claudius, given his recurring role across these relationships?

question:
Has **TestCorp** been linked to intermediaries, officers, or addresses in the same corporate network? Here’s the extracted data:

### **1. Same-Entity Connection**
- **Entity 1 (TestCorp)** → **Same-As Entity (e2)**
  - *Link*: Likely a formal registration or corporate tie (e.g., same jurisdiction, ownership structure).
  - **Result**: If `e2` is another entity with the same name or jurisdiction, they may share ownership, branches, or subsidiaries.

---

### **2. Officer Connections**
- **Entity 3 (Entity)** → **Officer (o1)** → **Connected-To Entity**
  - *Link*: Officer role (e.g., "shareholder," "director").
  - **Result**: If `e3` is TestCorp, its officers (e.g., executives) are linked to other entities via business ties, investments, or legal affiliations.

---

### **3. Intermediary Relationship**
- **Intermediary (i1)** → **Intermediary-Of Entity (e4)**
  - *Link*: "Intermediary of" (e.g., a front-person, trustee, or intermediary entity managing TestCorp’s affairs).
  - **Result**: If `i1` is a shell company or nominee, it may act as a proxy for TestCorp’s operations.

---

### **4. Registered Address**
- **Address (a1)** → **Registered-To Entity (e5)**
  - *Link*: Formal business address (e.g., registered office).
  - **Result**: If `e5` is TestCorp, its headquarters or primary contact is tied to this address.

---
### **Key Observations**
- **Potential Shadow Entities**: If `e2` (same-as) or `e4` (intermediary) are shell companies, they could be fronts for TestCorp’s activities.
- **Officer Networks**: Connected officers may share ties with other entities (e.g., related firms, family-owned businesses).
- **Address Consistency**: A registered address could indicate a corporate hub or compliance risks (e.g., hidden assets).

---
### **Actionable Insights**
- **Audit Intermediaries**: Investigate `i1` and `e4` for suspicious activities (e.g., tax evasion, money laundering).
- **Officer Due Diligence**: Check `o1` for conflicts of interest or shadow ownership.

MATCH
(

(

(
    m)-[r7:IN_COLLECTION]-
    col1:Collection,
(
    p:Person)-[r8:CAST_FOR]-m,
(
    p)-[r9:CREW_FOR]-m,
(

(
    v:Video)-[r11:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]-
    l3:Language,
(
    v)-[r12:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]-
    l4:Language,
(
    v)-[r13:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]-
    c2:Country,
(
    v)-[r14:IN_GENRE]-
    g2:Genre,
(
    v)-[r15:PRODUCED_BY]-
    pc2:ProductionCompany,
(
    v)-[r16:HAS_KEYWORD]-
    k2:Keyword,
(
    v)-[r17:IN_COLLECTION]-
    col2:Collection,
(

(

    l6:Language,
(

    c3:Country,
(
    a)-[r21:IN_GENRE]-
    g3:Genre,
(
    a)-[r22:PRODUCED_BY]-
    pc3:ProductionCompany,
(
    a)-[r23:HAS_KEYWORD]-
    k3:Keyword,
(
    p)-[r24:CREW_FOR]-v,
(
    u)-[r25:RATED]-v
WHERE
    DISTINCT(r1.credit_id) = DISTINCT(r9.credit_id) OR
    DISTINCT(r8.credit_id) = DISTINCT(r9.credit_id)
RETURN
    m.title AS movie_title,

    collect(distinct col2.title) AS video_collections,
    collect(distinct p.name) AS video_crew,
    collect(distinct u.username) AS video_users_rating,
    a.title AS adult_title,
    collect(distinct l5.name) AS adult_original_language,
    collect(distinct l6.name) AS adult_spoken_language,
    collect(distinct c3.name) AS adult_production_country,
    collect(distinct g3.name) AS adult_genres,
    collect(distinct pc3.name) AS adult_production_companies,
    collect(distinct k3.name) AS adult_keywords;
question:
Here’s the refined Cypher query based on your requirements to extract detailed metadata while ensuring proper filtering and deduplication. I’ll focus on a structured output for `Movie`, `Video`, and `Adult` content with their respective relationships:

```cypher
MATCH
    // Movie data
    (m:Movie)-[r1:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l1:Language),
    (m)-[r2:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l2:Language),
    (m)-[r3:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c1:Country),
    (m)-[r4:IN_GENRE]->(g1:Genre),
    (m)-[r5:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc1:ProductionCompany),
    (m)-[r6:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k1:Keyword),
    (m)-[r7:IN_COLLECTION]->(col1:Collection),

    // Cast and Crew
    (p:Person)-[r8:CAST_FOR]->m,
    (p)-[r9:CREW_FOR]->m,

    // User Ratings
    (u:User)-[r10:RATED]->m,

    // Video data
    (v:Video)-[r11:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l3:Language),
    (v)-[r12:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l4:Language),
    (v)-[r13:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c2:Country),
    (v)-[r14:IN_GENRE]->(g2:Genre),
    (v)-[r15:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc2:ProductionCompany),
    (v)-[r16:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k2:Keyword),
    (v)-[r17:IN_COLLECTION]->(col2:Collection),

    // Video Crew (excluding cast overlaps)
    (p)-[r18:CREW_FOR]->v,

    // User Ratings for Video
    (u)-[r19:RATED]->v,

    // Adult data
    (a:Adult)-[r20:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE]->(l5:Language),
    (a)-[r21:SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l6:Language),
    (a)-[r22:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(c3:Country),
    (a)-[r23:IN_GENRE]->(g3:Genre),
    (a)-[r24:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc3:ProductionCompany),
    (a)-[r25:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k3:Keyword),

WHERE
    // Filter overlapping cast/crew (if needed)
    DISTINCT(r8.credit_id) <> DISTINCT(r9.credit_id) OR
    DISTINCT(r18.credit_id) <> DISTINCT(r9.credit_id)

RETURN
    // Movie details
    m.title AS movie_title,
    collect(distinct l1.name) AS movie_original_language,
    collect(distinct l2.name) AS movie_spoken_language,
    collect(distinct c1.name) AS movie_production_country,
    collect(distinct g1.name) AS movie_genres,
    collect(distinct pc1.name) AS movie_production_companies,
    collect(distinct k1.name) AS movie_keywords,
    collect(distinct col1.title) AS movie_collections,

    // Cast and Crew
    collect(distinct p.name) AS movie_cast,
    collect(distinct p.name) AS movie_crew,

    // User Ratings
    collect(distinct u.username) AS movie_users_rating,

    // Video details
    v.title AS video_title,
    collect(distinct l3.name) AS video_original_language,
    collect(distinct l4.name) AS video_spoken_language,
    collect(distinct c2.name) AS video_production_country,
    collect(distinct g2.name) AS video_genres,
    collect(distinct pc2.name) AS video_production_companies,
    collect(distinct k2.name) AS video_keywords,
    collect(distinct col2.title) AS video_collections,

    // Video Crew (excluding cast overlaps)
    collect(distinct p.name) AS video_crew,

    // User Ratings for Video
    collect(distinct u.username) AS video_users_rating,

    // Adult details (no crew/ratings in this simplified version)
    a.title AS adult_title,
    collect(distinct l5.name) AS adult_original_language,
    collect(distinct l6.name) AS adult_spoken_language,
    collect(distinct c3.name) AS adult_production_country,
    collect(distinct g3.name) AS adult_genres,
    collect(distinct pc3.name) AS adult_production_companies,
    collect(distinct k3.name) AS adult_keywords
LIMIT 100;
```

### Key Improvements:
1. **Structured Output**: Separates `Movie`, `Video`, and `Adult` details clearly.
2. **Deduplication**: Uses `collect(distinct ...)` for unique values.
3. **Explicit Relationships**: Filters cast/crew relationships separately for each content type.
4. **Simplified Adult Data**: Focuses on metadata without crew/ratings (adjust as needed).
5. **LIMIT**: Added for performance (remove if not needed).

### Notes:
- Adjust `WHERE` conditions if you need to filter for specific overlaps (e.g., crew members).
- For `Adult` content, you may need to exclude crew/ratings if they are not applicable.
- If you need to include `Package`/`Subscription` relationships, you’ll need additional queries.

  a4 -[r20:officer_of]-> e2,
  a4 -[r21:similar]-> o1,
  a4 -[r22:intermediary_of]-> e1,

  a5 -[r23:same_as]-> a2,
  a5 -[r24:connected_to]-> e2,

  a6 -[r25:registered_address]-> a1,
  a6 -[r26:connected_to]-> e2,

  e2 -[r27:same_name_as]-> a1,
  e2 -[r28:same_name_as]-> a2,
  e2 -[r29:similar]-> o1,
  e2 -[r30:similar]-> a4,

  o1 -[r31:officer_of]-> e1,
  o1 -[r32:officer_of]-> e2,
  o1 -[r33:similar]-> a3,
  o1 -[r34:similar]-> i1,
  o1 -[r35:same_name_as]-> a3,
  o1 -[r36:same_as]-> i1,
  o1 -[r37:connected_to]-> e2,

  i1 -[r38:intermediary_of]-> e1,
  i1 -[r39:similar]-> o1,
  i1 -[r40:similar]-> a4,
  i1 -[r41:officer_of]-> e1,
  i1 -[r42:officer_of]-> e2,
  i1 -[r43:same_name_as]-> o1,
  i1 -[r44:same_name_as]-> a4,

  a2 -[r45:similar]-> o1

UNWIND [r1, r2, r3, r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11, r12, r13, r14, r15, r16, r17, r18, r19, r20, r21, r22, r23, r24, r25, r26, r27, r28, r29, r30, r31, r32, r33, r34, r35, r36, r37, r38, r39, r40, r41, r42, r43, r44, r45] AS r
RETURN
  a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6,
  e1, e2, o1, i1,
  collect(DISTINCT r.link) AS relationships,
  collect(DISTINCT r.sourceID) AS sources,
  collect(DISTINCT r.valid_until) AS valid_untils;
question:
Here’s a refined and optimized Cypher query to extract meaningful relationships for `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` and its associated nodes while avoiding redundant checks:

```cypher
MATCH
  // Target Entities
  e1 (e:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.'}),
  e2 (e:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.'}),

  // Intermediaries (potential parent entities)
  i1 (i:Intermediary),

  // Officers (direct roles)
  o1 (o:Officer),

  // Addresses (physical locations)
  a1 (a:Address {address: 'ANNEX FREDERICK & SHIRLEY STS, P.O. BOX N-4805, NA'})

WITH DISTINCT e1, e2, i1, o1, a1

// Match direct relationships (avoiding duplicates)
MATCH
  e1 -[r:registered_address|connected_to|same_as|same_company_as|intermediary_of|officer_of|similar] - n,
  e2 -[r2:registered_address|connected_to|same_as|same_company_as|similar] - n2,

  i1 -[r3:officer_of|similar|intermediary_of] - n3,

  o1 -[r4:officer_of|similar|same_name_as|connected_to] - n4,

  a1 -[r5:registered_address|connected_to] - n5

// Extract unique relationships
WITH e1, e2, i1, o1, a1,
     COLLECT(DISTINCT r) AS rels,
     COLLECT(DISTINCT r.sourceID) AS sources,
     COLLECT(DISTINCT r.valid_until) AS valid_untils

// Filter for relevant relationships
WHERE
  rels[?type in ['same_as', 'connected_to', 'officer_of']] OR
  rels[?type in ['same_company_as', 'intermediary_of']] OR
  rels[?type in ['similar', 'same_name_as']]

// Return clean results
RETURN
  e1, e2, i1, o1, a1,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT r.type) AS relationship_types,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT r.sourceID) AS sources,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT r.valid_until) AS valid_untils
```

### Key Improvements:
1. **Reduced Redundancy**:
   - Removed redundant `MATCH` clauses for `a2`/`a6` (addresses) since they’re already covered by `a1`.
   - Used `COLLECT(DISTINCT r)` to aggregate relationships cleanly.

2. **Focused Filtering**:
   - Explicitly checked for relevant relationship types (`same_as`, `officer_of`, etc.) to avoid unnecessary checks.

3. **Simplified Output**:
   - Grouped related properties (`sources`, `valid_untils`) for clarity.

### Expected Output Structure:
| Entity Type       | Node         | Relationship Types       | Sources            | Valid Until      |
|-------------------|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Entity (`e1`)     | `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` | `connected_to`, `officer_of`, `same_as` | `Paradise_Papers`, `Aruba` | `2022-12-31` |
| Officer (`o1`)    | `John Doe`   | `officer_of`, `similar` | `Paradise_Papers` | `2023-01-01` |

This query efficiently captures the core relationships while minimizing cycles.

MATCH
  p1 = (m:Movie)<-[r1:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]-(p:Person),
  p2 = (m)<-[r2:HAS_KEYWORD]-(k:Keyword),
  p3 = (m)<-[r3:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]-(c:Country),
  p4 = (m)<-[r4:IN_GENRE]-(g:Genre),
  p5 = (m)<-[r5:PRODUCED_BY]-(pc:ProductionCompany),
  p6 = (m)<-[r6:IN_COLLECTION]-(col:Collection),
  p7 = (m)<-[r7:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]-(lang:Language),
  p8 = (u:User)-[r8:RATED]->(m)<-[:Movie]-(u),
  p9 = (v:Video)<-[r9:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]-(p:Person),
  p10 = (v)<-[r10:HAS_KEYWORD]-(k:Keyword),
  p11 = (v)<-[r11:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]-(c:Country),
  p12 = (v)<-[r12:IN_GENRE]-(g:Genre),
  p13 = (v)<-[r13:PRODUCED_BY]-(pc:ProductionCompany),
  p14 = (v)<-[r14:IN_COLLECTION]-(col:Collection),
  p15 = (v)<-[r15:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]-(lang:Language),
  p16 = (a:Adult)<-[r16:HAS_KEYWORD]-(k:Keyword),
  p17 = (a)<-[r17:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]-(c:Country),
  p18 = (a)<-[r18:IN_GENRE]-(g:Genre),
  p19 = (a)<-[r19:PRODUCED_BY]-(pc:ProductionCompany),
  p20 = (a)<-[r20:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]-(lang:Language),
  p21 = (p:Person)-[:CREW_FOR]->(v:Video)<-[:Video]-(p)
WHERE
  NOT EXISTS(p1) OR NOT EXISTS(p2) OR NOT EXISTS(p3) OR NOT EXISTS(p4) OR NOT EXISTS(p5) OR NOT EXISTS(p6) OR NOT EXISTS(p7) OR
  NOT EXISTS(p8) OR NOT EXISTS(p9) OR NOT EXISTS(p10) OR NOT EXISTS(p11) OR NOT EXISTS(p12) OR NOT EXISTS(p13) OR NOT EXISTS(p14) OR
  NOT EXISTS(p15) OR NOT EXISTS(p16) OR NOT EXISTS(p17) OR NOT EXISTS(p18) OR NOT EXISTS(p19) OR NOT EXISTS(p20)
RETURN
  m.title AS movie_title,
  p.name AS person_name,
  k.name AS keyword,
  c.name AS country,
  g.name AS genre,
  pc.name AS production_company,
  col.name AS collection_name,
  lang.name AS language,
  u.username AS username,
  r8.rating AS movie_rating,
  v.title AS video_title,
  r9.order AS video_cast_order,
  r10.credit_id AS video_keyword_credit_id,
  r11.job AS video_producer_job,
  r13.department AS video_producer_department,
  a.title AS adult_title,
  r16.credit_id AS adult_keyword_credit_id,
  r17.job AS adult_producer_job,
  r19.department AS adult_producer_department;

WHERE NOT EXISTS(m)-[r:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]->()
RETURN m.title AS movie_title, d.name AS detail_info_type, d.name AS detail_value;
question:
Here’s a refined Cypher query that focuses on **missing relationships** (where `NOT EXISTS` is true) for movies, videos, and adults, while extracting relevant details from the connected nodes:

---

### **Query: Find Missing Relationships for Movies, Videos, and Adults**
```cypher
MATCH
  (m:Movie),
  (v:Video),
  (a:Adult),

  // --- Movies ---
  p1 = (m)-[r1:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]->(),
  p2 = (m)-[r2:HAS_KEYWORD]->(),
  p3 = (m)-[r3:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(),
  p4 = (m)-[r4:IN_GENRE]->(),
  p5 = (m)-[r5:PRODUCED_BY]->(),
  p6 = (m)-[r6:IN_COLLECTION]->(),
  p7 = (m)-[r7:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(),

  // --- Videos ---
  p8 = (v)-[r8:CAST_FOR|CREW_FOR]->(),
  p9 = (v)-[r9:HAS_KEYWORD]->(),
  p10 = (v)-[r10:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(),
  p11 = (v)-[r11:IN_GENRE]->(),
  p12 = (v)-[r12:PRODUCED_BY]->(),
  p13 = (v)-[r13:IN_COLLECTION]->(),
  p14 = (v)-[r14:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(),

  // --- Adults ---
  p15 = (a)-[r15:HAS_KEYWORD]->(),
  p16 = (a)-[r16:PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY]->(),
  p17 = (a)-[r17:IN_GENRE]->(),
  p18 = (a)-[r18:PRODUCED_BY]->(),
  p19 = (a)-[r19:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(),

  // --- Users who rated ---
  (u:User)-[r20:RATED]->(m)<-[:Movie]-(u),

  // --- Crew/actors for videos (if missing in movies) ---
  p21 = (p:Person)-[:CREW_FOR]->(v:Video)<-[:Video]-(p),

WHERE
  // Filter for missing relationships (e.g., CAST_FOR for movies)
  NOT EXISTS(p1) OR NOT EXISTS(p2) OR NOT EXISTS(p3) OR NOT EXISTS(p4) OR NOT EXISTS(p5) OR NOT EXISTS(p6) OR NOT EXISTS(p7) OR
  NOT EXISTS(p8) OR NOT EXISTS(p9) OR NOT EXISTS(p10) OR NOT EXISTS(p11) OR NOT EXISTS(p12) OR NOT EXISTS(p13) OR NOT EXISTS(p14) OR
  NOT EXISTS(p15) OR NOT EXISTS(p16) OR NOT EXISTS(p17) OR NOT EXISTS(p18) OR NOT EXISTS(p19) OR NOT EXISTS(p20) OR NOT EXISTS(p21)

RETURN
  // --- Movies ---
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p1), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_cast,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p2), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_keyword,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p3), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_country,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p4), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_genre,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p5), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_producer,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p6), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_collection,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p7), m.title, null) AS missing_movie_language,

  // --- Videos ---
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p8), v.title, null) AS missing_video_cast,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p9), v.title, null) AS missing_video_keyword,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p10), v.title, null) AS missing_video_country,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p11), v.title, null) AS missing_video_genre,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p12), v.title, null) AS missing_video_producer,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p13), v.title, null) AS missing_video_collection,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p14), v.title, null) AS missing_video_language,

  // --- Adults ---
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p15), a.title, null) AS missing_adult_keyword,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p16), a.title, null) AS missing_adult_country,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p17), a.title, null) AS missing_adult_genre,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p18), a.title, null) AS missing_adult_producer,
  IF(NOT EXISTS(p19), a.title, null) AS missing_adult_language,

  // --- Users who rated ---
  u.username AS username,
  r20.rating AS movie_rating,

  // --- Crew/actors for videos (if missing in movies) ---
  p21.person_name AS video_person_name,
  v.title AS video_title;
```

---

### **Key Improvements:**
1. **Focused on Missing Relationships**
   - Uses `NOT EXISTS` to identify missing properties (e.g., `CAST_FOR`, `HAS_KEYWORD`).
   - Returns `null` for existing relationships to avoid clutter.

2. **Separate Filters for Movies, Videos, Adults**
   - Avoids redundant checks by splitting paths for each entity type.

3. **Added User Ratings & Video Crew**
   - Includes `u.username` and `r20.rating` for context.
   - Retrieves missing crew/actors for videos (`p21`).

4. **Simplified Output**
   - Returns only relevant missing details (e.g., `missing_movie_cast` instead of raw paths).

---

### **Use Case:**
This query helps identify which movies, videos, or adults lack critical metadata (e.g., actors, genres, or production details). You can extend it further to populate missing relationships dynamically.

    p1 = (a:User {screen_name: 'neo4j'})-[r1:SIMILAR_TO]->(b:Me),

cypher:
MATCH
  e1 (Entity {name: "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V."}),
  e2 (Entity {name: "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V."}),
  e3 (Entity {name: "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V."}),
  i1 (Intermediary {name: "ANTAM ENTERPRISES INTERMEDIARY"}),
  o1 (Officer {name: "ANTAM OFFICER"}),
  a1 (Address {address: "ANTAM OFFICE ADDRESS"}),
  a2 (Address {address: "ANNEX FREDERICK & SHIRLEY STS, P.O. BOX N-4805, NA"}),
  o2 (Other {name: "ANTAM OTHER ENTITY"}),
  k1 (Other {name: "ANTAM COMPANY"}),
  r1 (registered_address {link: "registered address", valid_until: "The Bahamas Leaks data is current through early 20"}) --
  r2 (registered_address {link: "registered address", valid_until: "Aruba corporate registry data is current through 2"})
WHERE
  e1.name = "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V." OR
  e2.name = "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V." OR
  e3.name = "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V." OR
  e2.name = e3.name AND
  e2 -[:same_as]-> e1
OPTIONAL MATCH
  e1 -[:same_name_as]-> o2,
  e1 -[:same_name_as]-> k1,
  e2 -[:same_company_as]-> e3,
  e2 -[:same_id_as]-> o2,
  e2 -[:connected_to]-> o1,
  e2 -[:officer_of]-> o1,
  e2 -[r1]-> a1,
  e2 -[r2]-> a2,
  e3 -[r1]-> a1,
  e3 -[r2]-> a2,
  i1 -[:intermediary_of]-> e2,
  i1 -[r1]-> a1,
  i1 -[r2]-> a2,
  o1 -[similar]-> o1,
  o1 -[similar]-> i1,
  o1 -[officer_of]-> e3,
  o1 -[officer_of]-> o2,
  o1 -[r1]-> a1,
  o1 -[r2]-> a2,
  o2 -[r1]-> a1,
  o2 -[r2]-> a2,
  o2 -[:same_name_as]-> e1,
  o2 -[:same_name_as]-> k1,
  o2 -[:connected_to]-> e3,
  o2 -[:similar]-> o1,
  o2 -[:same_as]-> e3,
  k1 -[r1]-> a1,
  k1 -[r2]-> a2,
  k1 -[:same_name_as]-> e1,
  k1 -[:same_name_as]-> o2,
  k1 -[:same_name_as]-> o1,
  k1 -[:same_company_as]-> e3
RETURN
  e1.name AS entity_name,
  e2.name AS entity_alias,
  e3.name AS company_link,
  i1.name AS intermediary_name,
  o1.name AS officer_name,
  a1.address AS address_name,
  o2.name AS other_entity,
  k1.name AS similar_company,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e1) AS entity_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e2) AS alias_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT i1) AS intermediary_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o1) AS officer_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o2) AS other_entity_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT k1) AS similar_company_count;
question:
Here’s a refined and optimized Cypher query that avoids redundant patterns and ensures clarity while maintaining all the relationships you specified for **"ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V."** and its associated entities:

```cypher
MATCH
  e1 = (Entity {name: "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V."}),
  // Find alternative representations (same_as)
  e2 = ANY(e IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(e -[:same_as]-> e1)),
  // Find same_name_as and same_company_as links
  e3 = ANY(e IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(e -[:same_name_as]-> e1 OR e -[:same_company_as]-> e1)),
  // Intermediary (e.g., "ANTAM ENTERPRISES INTERMEDIARY")
  i1 = ANY(i IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(i -[:intermediary_of]-> e1 OR i -[:same_name_as]-> e1)),
  // Officer (e.g., "ANTAM OFFICER")
  o1 = ANY(o IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(o -[:officer_of]-> e1 OR o -[:similar]-> e1 OR o -[:same_name_as]-> e1)),
  // Addresses (e.g., "ANTAM OFFICE ADDRESS" or "ANNEX FREDERICK & SHIRLEY STS")
  a1 = ANY(a IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(e1 -[:registered_address]-> a OR o1 -[:registered_address]-> a OR i1 -[:registered_address]-> a)),
  a2 = ANY(a IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(a -[:same_as]-> a1)), // Cross-reference alternative addresses
  // Other entities (e.g., "ANTAM OTHER ENTITY" or "ANTAM COMPANY")
  o2 = ANY(o IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(o -[:same_name_as]-> e1 OR o -[:connected_to]-> e1 OR o -[:same_id_as]-> e1)),
  // Similar company (e.g., "ANTAM COMPANY")
  k1 = ANY(k IN e1 WHERE EXISTS(k -[:same_name_as]-> e1 OR k -[:same_company_as]-> e1)),
  // Ensure no duplicates
  DISTINCT e1, e2, e3, i1, o1, a1, o2, k1

OPTIONAL MATCH
  // Same_name_as relationships
  e1 -[:same_name_as]-> o2,
  e1 -[:same_name_as]-> k1,
  // Same_company_as relationships
  e1 -[:same_company_as]-> e3,
  // Intermediary connections
  i1 -[:intermediary_of]-> e1,
  i1 -[:registered_address]-> a1,
  // Officer connections
  o1 -[:officer_of]-> e1,
  o1 -[:officer_of]-> o2,
  o1 -[:registered_address]-> a1,
  // Similar relationships
  o1 -[:similar]-> o1,
  o1 -[:similar]-> i1,
  // Same_id_as (if applicable)
  o2 -[:same_id_as]-> o1,
  // Address cross-references
  e1 -[:registered_address]-> a1,
  e1 -[:registered_address]-> a2,
  a1 -[:same_as]-> a2,
  // Connected_to (if applicable)
  o2 -[:connected_to]-> e1,
  // Same_as (if applicable)
  o2 -[:same_as]-> e1

RETURN
  e1.name AS entity_name,
  e2.name AS alternative_entity_name,
  e3.name AS company_link,
  i1.name AS intermediary_name,
  o1.name AS officer_name,
  a1.address AS address_name,
  o2.name AS other_entity,
  k1.name AS similar_company,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e1) AS entity_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e2) AS alternative_entity_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e3) AS company_link_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT i1) AS intermediary_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o1) AS officer_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o2) AS other_entity_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT k1) AS similar_company_count;
```

### Key Improvements:
1. **Avoids Redundant Paths**: Uses `ANY` to fetch all relevant nodes (e.g., `e2`, `e3`, `o1`) without hardcoding all possible matches.
2. **Simplifies Logic**: Removes unnecessary `WHERE` clauses and consolidates `OPTIONAL MATCH` conditions.
3. **Handles Cross-Referencing**: Ensures addresses (`a1`, `a2`) and entities (`e1`, `e2`) are properly linked via `same_as` and `registered_address`.
4. **Counts Distinct Entities**: Accurately counts unique nodes (e.g., `entity_count`, `officer_count`).

### Example Output:
If the graph contains:
- `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` (e1)
- `ANTAM ENTERPRISES INTERMEDIARY` (i1)
- `ANTAM OFFICER` (o1)
- `ANTAM OFFICE ADDRESS` (a1)
- `ANTAM OTHER ENTITY` (o2)

The query will return:
```json
{
  "entity_name": "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.",
  "alternative_entity_name": null,
  "company_link": null,
  "intermediary_name": "ANTAM ENTERPRISES INTERMEDIARY",
  "officer_name": "ANTAM OFFICER",
  "address_name": "ANTAM OFFICE ADDRESS",
  "other_entity": "ANTAM OTHER ENTITY",
  "similar_company": null,
  "entity_count": 1,
  "alternative_entity_count": 0,
  "intermediary_count": 1,
  "officer_count": 1,
  "other_entity_count": 1
}
```

cypher:
MATCH
  a1 = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.'}),
  a2 = (:Other {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.'}),
  a3 = (:Address {address: 'ANNEX FREDERICK & SHIRLEY STS, P.O. BOX N-4805, NA'}),
  a4 = (:Intermediary {name: 'Fredderick Antam'}),
  a5 = (:Officer {name: 'Antam Shirley'})
WHERE
  a1.name = a2.name OR a2.name = a3.address OR a2.name = a4.name OR a4.name = a5.name
  AND a1.sourceID = a2.sourceID
  AND a1.sourceID = a3.sourceID
  AND a2.sourceID = a4.sourceID
  AND a3.sourceID = a5.sourceID
  AND a1.valid_until = a2.valid_until
  AND a2.valid_until = a3.valid_until
  AND a3.valid_until = a5.valid_until
  AND a1.sourceID = 'Bahamas Leaks'
  AND a1.valid_until = 'The Bahamas Leaks data is current through early 20'
WITH
  a1, a2, a3, a4, a5,
  COLLECT(a1) AS entities,
  COLLECT(a2) AS others,
  COLLECT(a3) AS addresses,
  COLLECT(a4) AS intermediaries,
  COLLECT(a5) AS officers
RETURN
  entities,
  others,
  addresses,
  intermediaries,
  officers,
  [
    (entity, 'same company as', other) |
    entity IN entities,
    other IN others,
    MATCH path = entity -[:same_company_as]-> other
  ],
  [
    (intermediary, 'officer of', entity) |
    intermediary IN intermediaries,
    MATCH path = intermediary -[:officer_of]-> entity
  ],
  [
    (address, 'registered address', intermediary) |
    address IN addresses,
    intermediary IN intermediaries,
    MATCH path = intermediary -[:registered_address]-> address
  ],
  [
    (intermediary, 'intermediary of', entity) |
    intermediary IN intermediaries,
    MATCH path = intermediary -[:intermediary_of]-> entity
  ],
  [
    (intermediary, 'similar', officer) |
    intermediary IN intermediaries,
    officer IN officers,
    MATCH path = intermediary -[:similar]-> officer
  ],
  [
    (officer, 'connected to', entity) |
    officer IN officers,
    MATCH path = officer -[:connected_to]-> entity
  ],
  [
    (entity, 'same name and registration date as', other) |
    entity IN entities,
    other IN others,
    MATCH path = entity -[:same_name_as]-> other
  ],
  [
    (officer, 'same name as', officer) |
    officer IN officers,
    MATCH path = officer -[:same_name_as]-> officer
  ]
LIMIT 20;  // Refined to focus on actual entity relationships

---

**Better Query (360° View of ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.):**

```cypher
MATCH
  // Core Entity with connections
  entity = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.'}),
  // Similar entities (same_name_as)
  similar_entities = entity -[:same_name_as]-> (:Entity),
  // Company connections
  same_company = entity -[:same_company_as]-> (:Entity),
  // Intermediaries
  intermediaries = entity <-[:officer_of]-, [:intermediary_of]-> (:Entity),
  // Addresses
  addresses = entity -[:registered_address]-> (:Address),
  // Officers
  officers = entity <-[:officer_of]-, [:similar]-> (:Officer),
  // Other entities (potential duplicates)
  other_entities = entity -[:same_name_as]-> (:Other)

RETURN
  entity,
  similar_entities,
  same_company,
  intermediaries,
  addresses,
  officers,
  other_entities,
  [
    (e, 'same name as', other) |
    e IN [entity, similar_entities, same_company],
    other IN [other_entities]
  ],
  [
    (inter, 'officer of', e) |
    inter IN intermediaries,
    e IN [entity, similar_entities, same_company]
  ],
  [
    (addr, 'registered address', e) |
    addr IN addresses,
    e IN [entity, intermediaries]
  ],
  [
    (off, 'connected to', e) |
    off IN officers,
    e IN [entity, same_company]
  ]
```

**Key Focus:**
- **Entity Core:** `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` and its direct company ties.
- **Name Matching:** Similar entities with identical names.
- **Intermediary Links:** Who serves as intermediaries/officers.
- **Addresses:** Where it’s registered.
- **Officers:** Key personnel connections.

This query avoids circular logic and focuses on **direct relationships** rather than transitive ones. Adjust `name` or `sourceID` as needed for real-world data.
question:
Here’s a refined Cypher query that extracts **all relevant relationships** for `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` while avoiding redundant traversals and focusing on meaningful connections:

```cypher
// Core Entity with source validation
MATCH
  entity = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.', sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'})<-[r:officer_of|connected_to|same_as]-(:Officer),
  entity = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.', sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'})<-[r2:officer_of|same_name_as]-(:Intermediary),
  entity = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.', sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'})-[r3:registered_address|same_company_as]-(:Address),
  same_name_entities = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.', sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'})-[r4:same_name_as]->(:Entity),
  same_company_entities = (:Entity {name: 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.', sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'})-[r5:same_company_as]->(:Entity)

// Extract all unique nodes and relationships
WITH
  DISTINCT entity,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT r) AS all_relationships,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.type IN ['officer_of', 'connected_to'] THEN (:Officer) ELSE NULL END) AS officers,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.type IN ['officer_of', 'similar'] THEN (:Intermediary) ELSE NULL END) AS intermediaries,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.type IN ['registered_address'] THEN (:Address) ELSE NULL END) AS addresses,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.type IN ['same_name_as'] THEN (:Entity) ELSE NULL END) AS same_name_entities,
  COLLECT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.type IN ['same_company_as'] THEN (:Entity) ELSE NULL END) AS same_company_entities

// Return structured relationships
RETURN
  entity AS 'ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.',
  [
    (entity, 'Officers', o) |
    o IN officers
  ],
  [
    (entity, 'Intermediaries', i) |
    i IN intermediaries
  ],
  [
    (entity, 'Addresses', a) |
    a IN addresses
  ],
  [
    (entity, 'Same Name Entities', sn) |
    sn IN same_name_entities
  ],
  [
    (entity, 'Same Company Entities', sc) |
    sc IN same_company_entities
  ],
  [
    (e, 'Type', r) |
    e IN [officers, intermediaries, addresses, same_name_entities, same_company_entities],
    r IN all_relationships,
    e <> entity // Exclude self-references
  ]
LIMIT 50;
```

### **Key Improvements:**
1. **Multi-source Validation**
   - Ensures `ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.` matches `sourceID: 'Bahamas Leaks'` across all entity types.

2. **Structured Relationship Extraction**
   - Separates **officers**, **intermediaries**, **addresses**, and **same-name/same-company entities** explicitly.

3. **Avoids Redundancy**
   - Uses `DISTINCT` to prevent duplicate nodes in results.
   - Focuses on **direct relationships** (e.g., `officer_of`, `registered_address`) rather than transitive ones.

4. **Readable Output**
   - Returns a nested list of relationships with clear labels (e.g., `Officers`, `Same Name Entities`).

### **Example Output:**
```json
{
  "ANTAM ENTERPRISES N.V.",
  [
    {"type": "Officers", "nodes": [Officer1, Officer2]},
    {"type": "Intermediaries", "nodes": [Intermediary1]},
    {"type": "Addresses", "nodes": [Address1]},
    {"type": "Same Name Entities", "nodes": [EntityX]},
    {"type": "Same Company Entities", "nodes": [CompanyY]}
  ]
}
```

### **Next Steps:**
- Filter for `valid_until` if needed (e.g., `entity.valid_until = 'The Bahamas Leaks data is current through early 20'`).
- Expand to include deeper traversals (e.g., `officers <-[:officer_of]-> :Entity`).

```cypher

// Rating

// Secondary attributes (redundant checks removed for efficiency)
WITH p, l, c, g, pc, k, col, p1, p2, u,

// Get distinct relationship types for the first set
[DISTINCT r1.type, DISTINCT r2.type, DISTINCT r3.type, DISTINCT r4.type, DISTINCT r5.type] AS distinctRelTypes,

// Filter for additional related entities (optional if you want all)
MATCH (p)-[r7:IN_COLLECTION]->(col2:Collection)
WITH p, l, c, g, pc, k, col, p1, p2, u, distinctRelTypes, col2

col2.name AS collection2
LIMIT 100;

### Key Adjustments:

2. **Simplified `MATCH` clauses** by focusing on core relationships.

WITH
    p,
    l.name AS language,
    c.name AS country,
    g.name AS genre,
    pc.name AS productionCompany,
    k.name AS keyword,
    col.name AS collection,
    p1.name AS castMember,
    p2.name AS crewMember,
    u.name AS rater,

    // Extract distinct relationship types (for Movies)
    DISTINCT r1.type AS languageType,
    DISTINCT r2.type AS countryType,
    DISTINCT r3.type AS genreType,
    DISTINCT r4.type AS productionCompanyType,
    DISTINCT r5.type AS keywordType,

    // Optional: Include collection relationship type (if uncommented)
    /*
    DISTINCT r7.type AS collectionType
    */

// Optional: If you want to analyze Videos (uncomment and modify)
/*
MATCH
    (p:Video)-[r1:ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE|SPOKEN_IN_LANGUAGE]->(l2:Language),
    (p)-[r2:PRODUCED_IN_COountry]->(c2:Country),
    (p)-[r3:IN_GENRE]->(g2:Genre),
    (p)-[r4:PRODUCED_BY]->(pc2:ProductionCompany),
    (p)-[r5:HAS_KEYWORD]->(k2:Keyword),
    (p)-[:IN_COLLECTION]->(col2:Collection),

WITH
    p,
    l2.name AS language,
    c2.name AS country,
    g2.name AS genre,
    pc2.name AS productionCompany,
    k2.name AS keyword,
    col2.name AS collection,

    DISTINCT r1.type AS languageType,
    DISTINCT r2.type AS countryType,
    DISTINCT r3.type AS genreType,
    DISTINCT r4.type AS productionCompanyType,
    DISTINCT r5.type AS keywordType
*/

// Final RETURN (for Movies)
RETURN
    p.name AS movieName,
    language,
    country,
    genre,
    productionCompany,
    keyword,
    collection,
    castMember,
    crewMember,
    rater,
    [languageType, countryType, genreType, productionCompanyType, keywordType] AS relationshipTypes,

// Optional: Include collection data (if uncommented)
/*
    col2.name AS collection2,
    [collectionType] AS collectionRelationshipType
*/
LIMIT 100;
```

---

### **Key Optimizations:**
1. **Removed redundant relationships** (e.g., `r6` for ratings is only used once).
2. **Used `WITH` to aggregate data efficiently** before returning.
3. **Explicitly named relationships** (e.g., `languageType` instead of `r1.type`).
4. **Conditional inclusion** of optional relationships (e.g., `IN_COLLECTION`).
5. **Uncomment the second block** to analyze **Videos** (modify `p:Video` and adjust return fields).

### **Output Example:**
| movieName       | language   | country    | genre        | productionCompany | keyword      | collection  | castMember | crewMember | rater         | relationshipTypes                     |
|-----------------|------------|------------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------------------------------------|
| "Inception"     | English    | USA        | Sci-Fi       | Warner Bros      | "Sci-Fi"     | Collection1 | John Doe    | Jane Smith  | Alice       | ["ORIGINAL_LANGUAGE", "PRODUCED_IN_COUNTRY", ...] |

This query efficiently retrieves all relationships while minimizing unnecessary traversals.

RETURN p1, p2, p3
question: How does the engagement pattern of DataSciFan compare to TechExplorer’s tweets from October 5, 2023, given their mutual high similarity score above 0.7?
